did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a convolutional neural network (CNN), a deep learning method, trained on 12,378 dermoscopic images (from ISIC and HAM10000 datasets) to classify melanoma in clinical images. The AI's performance (sensitivity and specificity) was directly compared with 145 dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'Original Research' in the header. It presents original experimental results from a study comparing a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on dermoscopic images against 145 dermatologists for melanoma classification, including specific methods, datasets (ISIC, HAM10000), and performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity).

title: A convolutional neural network trained with dermoscopic images performed on par with 145 dermatologists in a clinical melanoma image classification task
brief_summary: This study evaluated whether a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained exclusively on dermoscopic images could classify melanoma in clinical photographs at a level comparable to human dermatologists. The CNN was trained using 12,378 open-source dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive and the HAM10000 dataset. Its performance was compared against 145 dermatologists from 12 German university hospitals who classified 100 clinical images. The CNN achieved a mean specificity of 68.2% at the same sensitivity level as the dermatologists, who had a mean sensitivity of 89.4% and specificity of 64.4%. The results demonstrated that dermatologist-level classification is possible without training on clinical images, with the CNN showing smaller variance and higher robustness than human assessment.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for classification. In the training set (ISIC/HAM10000), there were 12,378 images total (1888 melanomas and 10,490 atypical nevi). In the test set (MClass-Benchmark), there were 20 biopsy-verified melanoma images.
The comparator class to melanoma. In the training set, there were 10,490 atypical nevi. In the test set, there were 80 nevi images declared as benign via expert consensus.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task where the CNN predicts the presence of melanoma in clinical images. The model outputs a continuous probability between 0 and 1, which is mapped to a discrete class label (melanoma or atypical nevus) using an operating value (threshold) to determine sensitivity and specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive and HAM10000 Dataset
datasets_used.description: Open-source dermoscopic image archives used for training and validation. The ISIC archive contained melanomas and atypical nevi verified via histopathology or expert consensus. The HAM10000 dataset is described as a large collection of multi-source dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions.

use.training: Used to train the convolutional neural network (CNN). The training set consisted of 12,378 images (1,888 melanomas and 10,490 atypical nevi).
use.validation: A validation set was created from the same source with a 1:10 ratio to training data, containing 230 melanomas and 1,129 atypical nevi. The test, training, and validation sets were disjoint.

datasets_used.name: MClass-Benchmark
datasets_used.description: A benchmark for clinical images originally obtained from the MED-NODE database. It consists of clinical close-up images of suspect lesions. The melanomas were biopsy-verified, and the nevi were declared benign via expert consensus.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the CNN and compare it with 145 dermatologists. The CNN and dermatologists classified these 100 clinical images.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize the performance (sensitivity and specificity) of the CNN and the 145 dermatologists on the test set.
To compare the performance of the CNN and dermatologists by plotting sensitivity against specificity and calculating the mean ROC curve.
To document the algorithm's performance by calculating the average predicted class over 50 runs of the CNN.
To compare the robustness of the CNN against human assessment by analyzing the variance of results among dermatologists versus the CNN.

To account for stochastic components in the CNN training and evaluation procedure (random splitting, gradient descent, parameter initialization) by reporting the first two moments of the distribution.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (mean sensitivity, specificity, and variance) and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves without statistical significance testing.

MClass-Benchmark (Test Set).description: 100 clinical images (20 biopsy-verified melanomas, 80 expert-consensus benign nevi) used to compare CNN performance against 145 dermatologists.

CNN (ResNet50).roc_auc: Not explicitly stated as a single number for CNN in text, but mean ROC curve shown in Fig. 2

MClass-Benchmark (Test Set).notes: The CNN exhibited a smaller variance of results compared to dermatologists, indicating higher robustness. Only 19 out of 145 dermatologists achieved higher sensitivity than the CNN given their individual specificity. In 16 of these 19 cases, dermatologists achieved an unusually high sensitivity of over 95%. The CNN outperformed attendings in specificity (61.4% vs 57.7%) at the same high sensitivity level (92.8%).

architecture_used.pretraining: Weights initialized from the same network architecture trained to classify images in the ImageNet data set

architecture_details.structure: Residual network with residual units and residual blocks
architecture_details.layer_groups: Split into three groups: first six residual units, subsequent eight residual blocks, and fully connected layers

training_details.loss_function: Loss function modeling the difference between predicted class labels and actual class labels
training_details.learning_rate_strategy: Differential learning rates applied to three layer groups; cosine annealing used to decrease learning rate stepwise

classification_or_output_head.interpretation: Probability that a melanoma was present in the input image
classification_or_output_head.decision_mechanism: Binary decision made by specifying an operating value (threshold) to trade off between sensitivity and specificity

preprocessing_techniques: Use of open-source dermoscopic images from ISIC archive and HAM10000 dataset
Use of clinical close-up images from MClass-Benchmark (originally from MED-NODE database)
Random selection of training and validation images using a random generator
Disjoint sets for training, validation, and testing
conclusions: A convolutional neural network (CNN) trained exclusively on dermoscopic images performed on par with 145 dermatologists in a clinical melanoma image classification task without being trained on clinical images.
The CNN achieved a mean specificity of 68.2% at the same sensitivity level (89.4%) as the dermatologists, who had a mean specificity of 64.4%.
The CNN exhibited a smaller variance of results compared to dermatologists, indicating higher robustness of computer vision compared with human assessment for dermatologic image classification tasks.
The study demonstrates that dermatologist-level image classification is possible without training on clinical images.
The CNN outperformed attending dermatologists in specificity (61.4% vs 57.7%) when operating at the same high sensitivity level (92.8%).

The training, validation, and test sets were derived from a mostly fair-skinned population, and rare melanoma subentities (such as amelanocytic melanomas) or lesions on dark-skinned patients were not present, representing a limitation for future research.
important_remarks: The study population and training data were derived from a mostly fair-skinned population; rare melanoma subentities (e.g., amelanocytic melanomas) and lesions on dark-skinned patients were absent, limiting generalizability.
The CNN was trained exclusively on dermoscopic images but tested on clinical close-up images, demonstrating that dermatologist-level performance is possible without training on clinical images.
The test set consisted of only 100 clinical images (20 melanomas, 80 nevi), which is a small sample size for evaluating diagnostic performance.

The study was the first stochastic analysis of its type, reporting the first two moments of the distribution to account for stochastic components in CNN training (random splitting, gradient descent, parameter initialization), mitigating the risk of adapting the algorithm to a known test set.

Only 19 out of 145 dermatologists achieved higher sensitivity than the CNN given their individual specificity; in 16 of these 19 cases, dermatologists achieved an unusually high sensitivity of over 95%.
The CNN exhibited smaller variance of results compared to dermatologists, indicating higher robustness of computer vision compared with human assessment.
The CNN outperformed attending dermatologists in specificity (61.4% vs 57.7%) when operating at the same high sensitivity level (92.8%).
The study compared the CNN against 145 dermatologists from 12 German university hospitals, exceeding the number of dermatologists in previous landmark publications (8, 21, or 58).
